author: @tehspamgozehere
text:
There are a few issues with the Rice method. Created by Tour's post-grad students, he stamped it with his name (which I'm told is tradition) and has been shouting he deserves a nobel for it. Unfortunately the graphene produced is still very poor quality. Research continues, of course, and there may yet be something to it, but those who have investigated it and looked beyond the hype have been largely unimpressed. At least that was how things looked when I checked in on it a year or two ago.

Still, even low grade or 'damaged' graphene has demonstrated some very interesting properties. I believe the guy over at Tech Ingredients tested the method and added the product to various plastics and epoxy samples and tested them for strength and durability and was quite impressed with the results. 

Personally, I just want to make decent electrodes and contacts for my experiments.

author: @RichardBronosky
text: I think the wet graphene making process has a scaling problem that you are the perfect person to improve upon. Imagine if instead of using a small spinning metal blade to do the shearing, you were to use... graphene. Take 2 water jets and shoot the streams into each other. Do it inside of a 55 gallon drum and the splatter will be contained inside. The feed hoses of the water jets draw water from the bottom of the drum. You just let it cycle the same mixture of water + precursor graphite until you've achieved the desired amount of sheared graphene conversion. You could now trickle new wet precursor in and an overflow drain would collect your finished product.

replies.author: @VRMediaI
replies.text: test this with varying temp of water to find the optimum shearing force. Logically this should be close to 4 C.

author: @Naomi_Boyd
text: I'm pretty sure that people have been making and using graphene for at least a couple of thousand years, to prevent eggs from sticking to their iron cookware.  Seriously, have you ever tried to sand the carbon off of an iron skillet?  It's nearly impervious.  The easiest way to remove it is to heat the iron until the thermal expansion of the metal forces the carbon to break loose, and then it comes off in big flakes, which is just another testament to the structural integrity of the carbon coating.

replies.author: @AnonymousAnarchist2
replies.text:
The 13 year long study across over 100 diffrent universities is still being compiled.
But what has been compiled and pisblished suggest that *clean* graphine and graphine oxide produced with biocompatable materials do get cleared from the body via the natural routes after a month or two. These studies even explicity cite the blender and whey method of production.
That could pose an extreme hazard for a full time worker, with build up over time and constant aggrivation but for at home use not so much. Simular to how Acetone (Nail polish remover) is extremly dangerious to industrial workers, but children are just fine to use it with no ill effects at home.

As far as lazed induced flash graphine, that is already bound, Unless your grinding it up and intentionally injceting it its not getting inside you.
But they did test that as well. It clumped, caused inflamation at the injection site and then was detected in the bladder before no detection about a month later.
